content.problemStatement:
Given an `N x M` matrix where each cell can have one of three values:


`0` representing an empty cell,
`1` representing a fresh apple, or
`2` representing a rotten apple.
Every minute, any fresh apple that is `4`-directionally adjacent to a rotten apple becomes rotten.

`4`-directionally adjacent cells of a cell (i, j) are cell (i - 1, j), (i + 1, j), (i, j - 1) and (i, j + 1).

Find the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh apple. If this is impossible, return -1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain two space separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the no. of rows and columns in the input matrix.
Next NNN lines contains MMM space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output on a single line, the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh apple. If this is impossible, output -1.
constraints: The elements of the matrix are either 0, 1 or 2
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers N and M, denoting the number of rows and columns in the input matrix. Next N lines contain M space-separated integers, the elements of the matrix.
content.outputFormat: Output on a single line, the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh apple. If this is impossible, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: There is a rotten apple in cell (0, 1) (assuming 0-based indexing). After 1 minute: fresh apples in cells (0, 2) and (1, 1) gets rotten. After 2 minutes: fresh apples in cells (0, 3), (1, 0) and (2, 1) gets rotten. After 3 minutes: fresh apples in cells (2, 0) and (1, 3) gets rotten. After 4 minutes: the last fresh apple in cell (2, 3) gets rotten. Hence it takes minimum 4 minutes to rot all apples.

Explanation: There is a rotten apple in cell (0, 0) (assuming 0-based indexing). After 1 minute: fresh apples in cells (0, 1) and (1, 0) gets rotten. After 2 minutes: fresh apples in cells (0, 2) and (1, 1) gets rotten. After 3 minutes: fresh apple in cell (2, 1) gets rotten. After 4 minutes: the last fresh apple in cell (2, 2) gets rotten. Hence it takes minimum 4 minutes to rot all apples.

Explanation: There is a rotten apple in cell (0, 0) (assuming 0-based indexing). After 1 minute: fresh apple in cell (0, 1) gets rotten. After 2 minutes: fresh apple in cell (0, 2) gets rotten. After 3 minutes: fresh apple in cell (0, 3) gets rotten. After 4 minutes: fresh apple in cell (0, 4) gets rotten. After 5 minutes: fresh apple in cell (0, 5) gets rotten. After 6 minutes: the last fresh apple in cell (0, 5) gets rotten. Hence it takes minimum 6 minutes to rot all apples.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm. We start by initializing a queue with all the rotten apples in the grid. Then, we perform BFS from each rotten apple, marking all the adjacent fresh apples as rotten and adding them to the queue. We repeat this process until the queue is empty. The time complexity of this approach is O(N*M), where N and M are the number of rows and columns in the grid, respectively.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N*M) per test case, O(T*N*M) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N*M) — the dominant structure is the queue used for BFS.
